author: @maxkennedy5073
text: Would love to see a comparison of output with the same generator on different turbines at various wind speeds.   You're right in that there is no 1 best design but for different windspeeds there will be a highest output design.  Then that info can be applied to the site you intend to set up in.  Of course there are other considerations, such as urban vs rural siting which will determine how smooth the windflow would be.

author: @SolwiseMD
text:
Permanent magnet motors are not ideal for variable speed power sources like turbines.   The 'lumpiness' you describe when you turn it prevents the turbine from starting in a low wind situation.  
What you need is a motor with an electro-magnet to energise the main coil(s) when it turns.  That way you can let the turbine start under no load, ie start with no energising current.  You could also find what energising current gives the optimum power output for each wind speed.  Have a word with your local tame motor mechanic to get one of these 'motors' out of a scrap car.  They call them alternators.

author: @anglosaxonmike8325

 of just 5% of our electricity.   Today, 10th June 2021, they are 
producing 0.87%.  Useless beyond words.

I hope you will like this idea of funneling of wind, please make such type of turbine also, I will be happy to see it..

author: @CitizenSmith50
text: So why are you building HAWT's with blades that taper outwards from the centre? Wouldn't blades with the blade width (and hence the mass) at the ends create a flywheel effect and capture more wind on smaller builds? Old water pumping mills here in Australia have blades wider at the perimeter than the centre!

replies.author: @paulbrouyere1735
replies.text: The problem with tail windturbines are the stresses caused by vibrations each time a blade passing the tower. This repeated downwind stresses may cause material fatigue. They can be designed that the rotor directs itself when wind direction changes. For large windturbines this automatic self regulation cannot be used because of the large stresses on the blades occurring with windshifts. I think it’s called coriolis forces, the same when you use an axle grinder and quickly change direction. It could demolish the turbine. That’s why large HAWT’s use yawing motors to adapt to changing wind directions in a controlled way.

author: @Gainn
text:
For my satellite dish Rose I'm using an really early E-scooter motor.. Seems to be doing pretty well and it's actually supplying more power than I need for the application I set it up for (3 water pumps for my pond). With a little boost circuit plus a small solar panel I'm getting enough to trickle charge 9 x 18650's to full even with the pumps running 24/7.
No idea of the actual spec, they were just a bunch of wheels with motors I picked up years ago. (will investigate)

replies.author: @Earzone63
replies.text: Is the boost circuit a joule thief or something else? Sounds brill.

replies.author: @Gainn
replies.text: @Earzone63 the electronics are boards from vape mods.. The input side is an evic mini with a boost circuit that Clive reverse engineered from a battery powered led set, and charging is handled by a wismec reuleaux output stage. It was a pain in the ass to get going and I had to get some help from a local that's been doing solar installations for years. I didn't think about using a joule thief.. I might be able to run one of those as a backup to keep the circuity alive if the power drops below their operating voltages.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is awesome - thank you for sharing they are 120W to 250W at 24V I think

author: @kilokilos

Traditional windmills are all over Africa and they work, but they are ugly , directional  and very expensive and are not allowed in most towns. Their blades are the size of the surface area of the circle they occupy , you cannot get more effective than that?

author: @malcolmbrown364
text: Hello Rob, Love the videos, very, very informative. It's probably the electrical engineer in me but I sometimes think the approach is backwards, in that the driver design (blades for example) is thought of first rather than the final output required. Once you know the output you require, to charge a set of batteries for instance, then you work backwards to achieve a driver design that will give you that output.

replies.author: @Gainn
replies.text:
The other approach is equally valid for experimentation.
Making something and then figuring out what you can do with it can sometimes provide inspiration you'd otherwise never get.
